methodology: Cross-reference network analysis (preexistence ŌåÆ kenosis ŌåÆ humiliation ŌåÆ exaltation arc) + patristic commentary reading (Philippians 2:7 = 41 commentaries ŌĆö absolute record in Catena Bible; Augustine, Gregory of Nyssa, Leo of Rome, Hilary, Chrysostom, Cyril) + CCEL works (Calvin) + topic verification (HUMILITY_OF_CHRIST, SELF_DENIAL, CONDESCENSION_OF_GOD)

evidence: commentaries (41 entries! ŌĆö absolute record: Augustine, Gregory of Nyssa, Leo of Rome, Hilary, Chrysostom, Cyril)
commentaries (13 entries ŌĆö Chrysostom cites Isaiah 53:12)
commentaries (10 entries ŌĆö Augustine cites Phil 2:6-7 directly, Chrysostom 'poverty productive of riches')
commentaries (7 entries ŌĆö Augustine 'predestined glory', Lapide 'threefold glory')

relevance_change.reason: Establishes the PRE-EXISTENCE glory without which kenosis makes no theological sense. Lapide articulates 'threefold glory' (uncreated, created, manifested). Augustine connects predestination to glorification. 7 votes to PHP.2.6.

new_refs_added.justification: 13 votes para MAT.11.29. Introductory verse that frames kenosis as ETHICAL MODEL: 'Have this mind among yourselves, which is yours in Christ Jesus.' Topic HUMILITY_OF_CHRIST. Without it, the kenosis passage lacks its practical application.

8 votes de PHP.2.6. 7 votes de PHP.2.7. 19 votes para 1TI.3.16. The incarnation verse ŌĆö kenosis in narrative action: 'The Word became flesh and dwelt among us.' The most concrete description of what 'emptying himself' looks like.

8 votes para HEB.2.9 and MAT.28.18. 12 votes para ISA.45.23. Athanasius: 'exaltation is of the humanity, not of the divine nature.' Completes the kenosisŌåÆexaltation arc. Topic HUMILITY_OF_CHRIST: 'His exaltation, the result of.'

candidates_noted.justification: 7 votes de PHP.2.7. Concrete incarnation: 'born of a woman, born under the law' = form of servant in Pauline terms.
Chrysostom cites Is 53:12 in Phil 2:8 commentary. Topic HUMILITY_OF_CHRIST lists Is 53:3,7. OT prefiguration of the emptying.

patristic_consensus_on_kenosis.description: Unanimous patristic formula confirmed by 41 commentators on Philippians 2:7
patristic_consensus_on_kenosis.formula: kenosis = ADDITION of humanity, NOT SUBTRACTION of divinity

key_articulations.formula: 'Emptied himself' by TAKING the form of a servant, not by LOSING the form of God. The nature by which he is equal to the Father remained immutable.
The ineffable glory CONTRACTED within our small compass. What he was remained great and incomprehensible; what he assumed was commensurate with our nature.
A sovereign act of stooping in majestic pity, NOT a defect of power.
Not changed but 'emptying himself' and hiding WITHIN himself.
Had he been subjected without choosing it, it would not have been humility. He had equality not by seizure but by nature.

what_was_emptied: Status and appearance of divine equality

record_commentary_count.note: Absolute record in the entire Catena Bible corpus across all queries validated. The kenosis verse attracted more patristic commentary than any other single verse examined in this research.

kenosis_exaltation_arc.description: Complete theological arc confirmed by cross-references and commentaries
stages: 1. Preexistence: Jn 17:5, Phil 2:6 ŌĆö 'glory before the world', 'form of God'
2. Kenosis: Phil 2:7, 2Co 8:9 ŌĆö 'emptied himself', 'being rich, became poor'
3. Incarnation: Jn 1:14, Gal 4:4 ŌĆö 'Word became flesh', 'born of woman'
4. Humiliation: Phil 2:8, Heb 2:9 ŌĆö 'obedient unto death, even death on a cross'
5. Exaltation: Phil 2:9-11, Is 45:23 ŌĆö 'God highly exalted him', 'every knee shall bow'
